name: Azure Cognitive Services Provisioning and Management
description: Planning, deploying, and managing Azure Cognitive Services resources including multi-service and single-service accounts.
key_facts: Cognitive Services accounts can be multi-service (all APIs) or single-service (one specific API)
Authentication uses subscription keys or Azure AD tokens; keys are preferred for service-to-service calls
Diagnostic logging can be sent to Log Analytics, Storage Account, or Event Hubs
Virtual Network Service Endpoints and Private Endpoints restrict access to specific networks
Cost management uses resource tags, budgets, and Azure Cost Management dashboards
common_misconceptions: Developers assume a single Cognitive Services key works across all regions—each resource is region-specific
Many believe multi-service accounts are always cheaper, but single-service accounts can be more cost-effective for high-volume single API use

name: Azure OpenAI Service Deployments
description: Deploying, configuring, and managing Azure OpenAI models including GPT-4, GPT-3.5 Turbo, and embedding models.
key_facts: Azure OpenAI requires an approved subscription; access is not available by default
Models are deployed as 'deployments' within an Azure OpenAI resource with custom deployment names
The Chat Completions API uses a messages array with system, user, and assistant roles
Token limits include both prompt tokens and completion tokens; total must not exceed model context window
Content filtering policies can be customized per deployment to adjust severity thresholds
common_misconceptions: Developers assume Azure OpenAI models are identical to OpenAI.com models—Azure versions may lag behind public releases
Many believe you can use a standard OpenAI API key with Azure OpenAI—Azure requires its own endpoint and key format

name: Prompt Engineering and System Messages
description: Designing effective prompts and system messages to guide Azure OpenAI model behavior, including few-shot learning and chain-of-thought techniques.
key_facts: System messages define the AI assistant persona, constraints, and behavioral guidelines
Few-shot prompting provides examples within the prompt to guide output format and quality
Temperature (0-2) controls randomness; lower values produce more deterministic outputs
Top_p (nucleus sampling) is an alternative to temperature; use one or the other, not both
Chain-of-thought prompting improves reasoning by asking the model to 'think step by step'
common_misconceptions: Many assume longer system messages always improve accuracy—overly long system messages can dilute key instructions
Developers set both temperature and top_p together thinking it gives more control—Microsoft recommends setting only one at a time

name: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Azure AI Search
description: Implementing RAG patterns using Azure AI Search as the vector store to ground Azure OpenAI responses in proprietary data.
key_facts: RAG retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base and injects them into the prompt context
Azure AI Search supports vector search using text-embedding-ada-002 or text-embedding-3 models
The 'Add your data' feature in Azure OpenAI Studio configures RAG with minimal code
Chunking strategy affects retrieval quality; overlapping chunks improve context continuity
Semantic ranker re-ranks search results using language understanding to improve relevance
common_misconceptions: Teams assume RAG eliminates hallucinations entirely—it reduces but does not eliminate them when retrieved context is incomplete
Many believe RAG requires fine-tuning the model—RAG is a prompting technique that works with base models

name: Azure Content Safety Service
description: Detecting and moderating harmful content across text and images using Azure Content Safety, including custom blocklists and severity scoring.
key_facts: Azure Content Safety detects four harm categories: Hate, SelfHarm, Sexual, and Violence
Each category returns a severity score from 0 to 6 (Safe, Low, Medium, High)
Custom blocklists allow organization-specific term filtering beyond the default categories
The Groundedness detection feature checks if AI responses are grounded in provided source material
Content Safety Studio provides a no-code interface to test and configure moderation settings
common_misconceptions: Developers assume Content Safety is automatically applied to Azure OpenAI—it requires explicit integration or enabling content filters on the deployment
Many believe blocklists work across all languages equally—some languages have lower accuracy than English

name: Azure AI Vision Image Analysis
description: Analyzing images using Azure AI Vision to extract captions, tags, objects, faces, and OCR text from images.
key_facts: Image Analysis 4.0 uses Florence foundation model for improved accuracy over previous versions
The Analyze API accepts image URLs or binary data and returns structured JSON results
Visual features include: Caption, DenseCaptions, Tags, Objects, Read (OCR), SmartCrops, People
Dense Captions generates multiple region-specific captions with bounding box coordinates
Background removal is a new capability in Image Analysis 4.0 available via the segment endpoint
common_misconceptions: Developers assume the Read (OCR) feature in Image Analysis replaces the dedicated Read API—for large documents, the dedicated Document Intelligence service is more appropriate
Many believe Image Analysis works equally well for all image resolutions—images must be between 50x50 and 16000x16000 pixels

name: Custom Vision Image Classification and Object Detection
description: Training and deploying custom image classification and object detection models using Azure Custom Vision service.
key_facts: Custom Vision supports two project types: Image Classification (multi-class or multi-label) and Object Detection
Minimum training images: 5 per tag for classification, 15 per tag for object detection
Trained models can be exported as ONNX, TensorFlow, CoreML, or Docker containers for edge deployment
Smart Labeler uses the trained model to suggest tags for unlabeled images, reducing labeling time
Iterations represent trained model versions; you publish a specific iteration to make it available via the prediction API
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Custom Vision requires hundreds of images to train—the service uses transfer learning and achieves good results with as few as 15-50 images per class
Many believe exporting to ONNX gives identical accuracy to the cloud API—exported models may have slightly different accuracy due to model compression

name: Azure AI Language Service Features
description: Implementing NLP capabilities including sentiment analysis, key phrase extraction, named entity recognition, and language detection using Azure AI Language.
key_facts: Azure AI Language consolidates Text Analytics, LUIS, QnA Maker, and other NLP services into one API
Sentiment Analysis returns document-level and sentence-level sentiment with confidence scores
Named Entity Recognition (NER) identifies entities in 9 categories including Person, Location, and DateTime
Personally Identifiable Information (PII) detection identifies and redacts sensitive data
The API supports batch processing of up to 25 documents per request with async for larger volumes
common_misconceptions: Developers assume Language Detection works on short strings with equal accuracy—very short texts (under 20 characters) may return lower confidence scores
Many believe Sentiment Analysis always returns a binary positive/negative result—the service returns Positive, Negative, Neutral, or Mixed at both document and sentence levels

name: Conversational Language Understanding (CLU)
description: Building intent recognition and entity extraction models using Conversational Language Understanding, the successor to LUIS.
key_facts: CLU is the next-generation replacement for LUIS (Language Understanding Intelligent Service)
Intents represent user goals; entities extract specific information from utterances (e.g., city name, date)
Multilingual projects train on one language and can predict in 96+ languages without retraining
Orchestration workflow projects can route to CLU, QnA, or other language projects
Active learning suggests utterances to review and label based on low-confidence predictions
common_misconceptions: Teams assume LUIS apps must be rebuilt entirely for CLU—Microsoft provides migration tools to convert LUIS apps to CLU format
Many believe CLU requires large datasets—CLU uses few-shot learning and achieves good results with 15-25 utterances per intent

name: Azure Bot Service and Bot Framework
description: Building, testing, and deploying intelligent bots using Azure Bot Service and the Bot Framework SDK.
key_facts: Bot Framework SDK supports C# and JavaScript/TypeScript; Python is also available but less common
Dialogs manage conversation flow; Waterfall Dialogs, Component Dialogs, and Adaptive Dialogs are the main types
The Bot Framework Emulator allows local testing of bots without deploying to Azure
Channels include Teams, Slack, WhatsApp, SMS, and Direct Line for custom integrations
Azure Bot Service handles channel registration and authentication; the bot logic runs in Azure App Service or Azure Functions
common_misconceptions: Developers assume bots must be hosted in Azure App Service—bots can run anywhere that can receive HTTPS POST requests including Azure Functions or on-premises
Many believe Direct Line is only for web chat—Direct Line is a flexible channel for any custom application including mobile apps and IoT devices

name: Azure AI Document Intelligence
description: Extracting structured data from documents using prebuilt and custom models in Azure AI Document Intelligence (formerly Form Recognizer).
key_facts: Prebuilt models include Invoice, Receipt, ID Document, Business Card, W-2, and general document models
Custom models require labeled training data; the service supports template and neural model types
The Read model extracts text and handwriting from documents with layout information
Composed models combine multiple custom models to handle different document formats with a single API call
Document Intelligence Studio provides a web-based interface for labeling, training, and testing models
common_misconceptions: Teams assume prebuilt models work perfectly for all document variants—prebuilt models are trained on common formats and may need custom models for non-standard layouts
Many believe the Read model and the OCR feature in Vision are the same—Document Intelligence's Read model includes layout analysis (tables, paragraphs) while basic OCR extracts only text

name: Azure AI Search Knowledge Mining
description: Building knowledge mining solutions using Azure AI Search with AI enrichment pipelines to extract insights from unstructured content.
key_facts: AI enrichment uses skillsets that chain cognitive skills to transform and enrich documents during indexing
Built-in skills include OCR, entity recognition, key phrase extraction, sentiment, image analysis, and language detection
Custom skills extend the pipeline via Azure Functions that call any external API or ML model
Knowledge stores persist enriched data to Azure Storage for independent analysis or downstream use
Indexers automate data ingestion from sources including Blob Storage, SQL, Cosmos DB, and SharePoint
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Azure AI Search only does full-text search—it also supports vector search, semantic ranking, and knowledge mining with AI enrichment
Many believe skillsets process data in parallel—skills in a skillset execute sequentially in dependency order

name: Azure AI Speech Service
description: Implementing speech-to-text, text-to-speech, speech translation, and speaker recognition using Azure AI Speech.
key_facts: Speech-to-text supports real-time transcription and batch transcription for pre-recorded audio
Custom Speech allows fine-tuning the base model with domain-specific vocabulary and acoustic models
Text-to-speech supports 400+ voices in 140+ languages; custom neural voices can be created from recordings
Speech translation enables real-time translation of speech in one language to text in another language
The Speech SDK supports continuous recognition for long-running sessions with automatic segmentation
common_misconceptions: Developers assume the Speech SDK and the REST API offer identical features—some features like continuous recognition are only available in the SDK
Many believe Custom Speech requires thousands of hours of audio—Custom Speech can improve accuracy significantly with 30-60 minutes of representative audio

name: Microsoft Responsible AI Principles
description: Applying Microsoft's six Responsible AI principles to AI solution design and governance.
key_facts: Microsoft's six RAI principles are: Fairness, Reliability & Safety, Privacy & Security, Inclusiveness, Transparency, and Accountability
Fairness requires AI systems to treat all people equitably and avoid bias across demographic groups
Azure Machine Learning's Responsible AI dashboard integrates fairness assessment, error analysis, and explainability
The HAX (Human-AI Experience) Toolkit provides design guidelines for human-AI interaction
Impact assessments should be conducted before deploying high-stakes AI systems affecting people's lives
common_misconceptions: Many assume Responsible AI is only a compliance checkbox—Microsoft embeds RAI requirements into service terms and some features require compliance documentation
Teams believe AI fairness means equal accuracy across all groups—fairness also considers disparate impact and equal opportunity metrics

name: Azure AI Translator Service
description: Implementing text translation, transliteration, language detection, and custom translation models using Azure AI Translator.
key_facts: Azure Translator supports 100+ languages for text translation and 75+ languages for document translation
Document Translation translates entire documents while preserving the original layout and formatting
Custom Translator allows training custom translation models using parallel sentence pairs for domain-specific terminology
The Translate API can detect the source language automatically when the from parameter is omitted
Profanity filtering options include: NoAction (leave as-is), Deleted (remove), or Marked (tag with XML)
common_misconceptions: Developers assume Azure Translator and Azure Speech translation use the same underlying model—they are separate services optimized for different modalities
Many believe custom translation models require thousands of sentence pairs—Custom Translator can improve quality with as few as 10,000 parallel sentences
